summary: Stellar soundboard capture of a hot 1981 show with scorching Let It Grow, Cumberland Blues and a double‑time C.C. Rider, despite minor pitch quirks.
review: The Miller SBD delivers clear, crisp audio that most listeners describe as pristine, though a few note slight speed/pitch anomalies and occasional muffed moments. Musically the concert shines – Garcia’s hot tone on Let It Grow and Cumberland, Brent’s wizardry on Shakedown, and a memorable double‑time C.C. Rider make this a standout early‑80s performance, with only minor slips such as a mangled Sugar Magnolia.

ai_rating.rationale: Over two dozen reviews overwhelmingly rate the show 4–5 stars and praise both performance and recording; only a handful of mixed comments keep the rating just below top‑tier.

recording_quality.technical_notes: Generally described as crisp, stellar and pristine. A few reviewers mention slight pitch/speed errors and occasional muffed sections in the audience mix, but overall the SBD is very listenable.

show_quality.setlist_flow: First set builds with strong openers (Cumberland, Let It Grow) and a surprise double‑time C.C. Rider that energizes the band. The second set delivers extended jams – Shakedown > Lost Sailor > Saint, a big Railroad Blues, and a Playin' sandwich capped by Space‑filled Wheel – keeping momentum high despite a few rough patches.

band_member_comments.Jerry: Garcia’s playing is repeatedly praised – hot tone on Let It Grow and Cumberland, superb solos on Shakedown; one reviewer notes his mix can sound low or overly bright.
band_member_comments.Phil: Lesh is highlighted for the spontaneous double‑time cue on C.C. Rider and provides solid bass throughout.
band_member_comments.Bob: Weir’s vocals are mentioned only in a negative context (mangled start of Sugar Magnolia); otherwise not a focus.
band_member_comments.Keys: Brent Mydland receives high marks for his work on Shakedown, the double‑time C.C. Rider, and overall keyboard texture, though one reviewer felt his mix was too forward/bright.
band_member_comments.Drums: Bill Kreutzmann (and occasionally Mickey Hart) receive generic praise (“drumz”) but no detailed commentary.
